teaching.title: Classic for Loop in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ inherits C's for loop perfectly — same three-part structure, same semicolons. But C++ adds extra safety with its type system and eliminates the sizeof hack needed in C for array lengths.
teaching.content: for (init; condition; update) { body } — identical to C and Java. C++ adds: ranged-based for (for-each), auto in the init, and better type safety. All three parts optional; for(;;) is infinite loop.

examples.title: Classic C++ for Loop
examples.explanation: Identical syntax to C and Java.

examples.title: auto in for loop (C++11)

teaching.funFact: C++11 introduced auto to the for loop — and it was so popular it kickstarted auto everywhere in modern C++. Now auto is used for variables, return types, lambdas, and more! 🚀

teaching.title: Range-Based for Loop (C++11)
teaching.analogy: C++'s range-based for is the most elegant loop in the language — no index, no bounds, automatic type. Like telling a sommelier 'serve everyone at the table' instead of 'serve person 0, then 1, then 2...'
teaching.content: for (auto element : container) — iterates every element. Works on arrays, vectors, strings, maps, sets, and any range. Use 'const auto&' for efficiency with large objects. Use 'auto&' to modify elements.

examples.title: Range-based for with auto
examples.explanation: The cleanest way to iterate in C++.

examples.title: Modifying with auto&

teaching.funFact: const auto& in for loops is a C++ performance best practice — it avoids copying large objects on each iteration. Without &, the loop copies every element. For strings and vectors, this matters a lot! ⚡

mcq.q: for(auto x : arr) — what is x on each iteration?
mcq.explanation: Copy by Value: for(auto x : arr) gives x a COPY of each element. To avoid copying (for efficiency) or to modify, use auto& instead.

mcq.q: To MODIFY elements during range-based for, use:
mcq.explanation: Reference: auto& x binds a reference to each element — allowing in-place modification. Without &, you modify a copy that's thrown away. const auto& prevents modification but avoids copies.

mcq.q: for (const auto& s : strings) — why const auto&?
options: Avoids copying large strings on each iteration while preventing modification
Makes loop faster by using GPU
mcq.explanation: Efficiency: const auto& binds a const reference — no copy made. For large objects like strings or vectors, this is significantly faster than auto (which copies).

mcq.q: C++ range-based for requires which C++ standard minimum?
mcq.explanation: C++11: Range-based for was introduced in C++11 (2011). Compile with -std=c++11 or later. Most modern compilers default to C++14 or C++17.

mcq.q: int arr[]={1,2,3}; for(auto x:arr) sum+=x; What is sum?

coding.title: Range-based syntax
coding.objective: Complete: for (___ x : nums) { cout << x; }
coding.starterCode:
vector<int> nums = {1,2,3};
for (___ x : nums) { cout << x; }
coding.hint: Use auto to let C++ deduce the element type automatically.

coding.title: Modify with ref
coding.objective: To double each element in-place: for(auto___ n : nums) n*=2;
coding.starterCode:
for(auto___ n : nums) n *= 2;
// What fills the blank to enable modification?
coding.hint: auto& creates a reference — modifications affect the original element.

coding.title: Const ref benefit
coding.objective: for(const auto& s : strings) avoids what per iteration?
coding.starterCode: // const auto& avoids making a ___ of each string element
coding.hint: Without &, each element is copied into x. With &, it binds a reference — no copy.

options: auto n copies each element — modifying n doesn't affect v. Use: for(auto& n : v)
debugging.answer: auto n copies each element — modifying n doesn't affect v. Use: for(auto& n : v)
debugging.hint: auto n = copy. auto& n = reference. You need & to modify the original.

options: i-- decrements — i goes negative, stays < 5 forever. Change to i++
debugging.answer: i-- decrements — i goes negative, stays < 5 forever. Change to i++
debugging.hint: i-- makes i smaller each time. It will always satisfy i < 5 → infinite loop.

teaching.title: while Loop in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ while is identical to C's while — same syntax, same semantics. But C++ benefits: the condition can use bool naturally, and you can use C++ objects (like checking if a file is open) as conditions via operator overloading.
teaching.content: while (condition) { body } — condition tested before each iteration. C++ makes bool a first-class type, so while (myFile.is_open()) works elegantly. while(true) is preferred over while(1) in C++.

examples.title: C++ while Loop
examples.explanation: while(true) is idiomatic C++ (vs C's while(1)).

teaching.funFact: while(true) is preferred over while(1) in C++ because C++ has a proper bool type and true/false constants. It's more readable and expresses intent clearly. Clang-tidy and GCC warn about while(1) in C++ mode! 🎯

teaching.title: do-while in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ do-while is inherited from C — run once, then check. C++ enhancement: the condition can use C++ objects and bool naturally.
teaching.content: do { body } while (condition); — body always runs at least once. Semicolon required. Use for menus, input validation. C++ can use object conditions: do { } while (!file.eof());

examples.title: C++ do-while

teaching.funFact: C++ iterators from the STL can be used in while loops elegantly: while (it != container.end()) { process(*it); ++it; } This is how C++ standard algorithms work internally! 🔄

mcq.q: In C++, which is idiomatic for an infinite loop?
mcq.explanation: while(true): C++ has native bool — true is preferred over 1. while(true) expresses intent and is the idiomatic C++ infinite loop.
Once: do-while always executes body first. Then checks while(false) — false, stops. Prints 'x' once.

options: while may run 0 times; do-while runs at least once
while is C++, do-while is C
mcq.explanation: Key: while checks FIRST (may run 0 times). do-while checks AFTER (runs at least once). Choose based on whether one initial run is guaranteed.

options: C++ has native bool — true is clearer than 1
mcq.explanation: Clarity: C++ has bool type with true/false literals. Using true expresses intent better than 1. Linters prefer while(true) in C++ code.

coding.title: C++ infinite loop
coding.objective: Fill blank for idiomatic C++ infinite loop.
coding.hint: C++ prefers the bool literal 'true' over the integer 1.

coding.title: do-while runs once
coding.hint: Body always runs once before condition is checked.

options: Missing x-- inside loop — x never decreases, condition stays true forever!
debugging.answer: Missing x-- inside loop — x never decreases, condition stays true forever!
debugging.hint: Always update the loop variable inside while. Add x--; to the loop body.

teaching.title: break, continue & Nested Loops
teaching.analogy: break and continue work identically in C, C++, and Java. But C++ adds a modern touch: with algorithms and lambdas, you often don't need break/continue at all — filter and find do it for you.
teaching.content: break exits the innermost loop. continue skips to next iteration. C++ does NOT have goto like C (well it does but it's heavily discouraged even more so). Modern C++ uses STL algorithms: std::find, std::any_of, std::all_of.

examples.title: break and continue in C++
Modern C++ alternative

teaching.funFact: Modern C++ (C++11 and later) philosophy: prefer algorithms over raw loops. std::find, std::sort, std::transform, std::accumulate eliminate most manual loop code. The C++ Core Guidelines explicitly recommend this! 📚

mcq.q: break in a C++ range-based for loop:
options: Works identically to classic for — exits the loop
mcq.explanation: break works in ALL C++ loops — classic for, range-based for, while, do-while. It exits the current loop immediately.

mcq.q: continue in range-based for does what?
options: Skips rest of this iteration, moves to next element
mcq.explanation: continue: Skips rest of current iteration's body. Next iteration begins with the next element in the range.

options: It's heavily discouraged — use exceptions/algorithms instead
mcq.explanation: goto in C++: Present but heavily frowned upon. C++ has exceptions for error handling and algorithms for early exits — making goto unnecessary in well-written C++ code.
Iterates init-list. Prints 1, 3. When x=5 → break exits. Output: 1 3.

coding.title: break exit value
coding.starterCode: // Prints 1,2 then breaks at x=3. Last: ___
coding.hint: Prints 1, 2. At x=3 → break. Last print: 2.

options: auto x copies — modifying x doesn't affect v. Use: for(auto& x : v)
debugging.answer: auto x copies — modifying x doesn't affect v. Use: for(auto& x : v)
debugging.hint: auto x = copy of element. auto& x = reference to element. Need & to modify v.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE OPTIMIZER
teaching.analogy: The C++ Optimizer unrolls your loops at compile time, vectorizes iterations, and eliminates redundant copies. Defeat it by writing loops that are correct, efficient, and modern.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 3 C++: classic for, range-based for with auto/auto&/const auto&, while(true), do-while semicolon, break/continue in all loop types, nested loop total counts, const auto& efficiency.

examples.title: Full C++ Loop Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ for (int i=0; i<n; i++)
✅ for (auto x : container) — by value
✅ for (auto& x : container) — modify
✅ for (const auto& x : container) — efficient read
✅ while(true) + break
✅ do { } while(cond); — semicolon!
✅ break/continue in any loop
✅ C++11 required for range-based for

teaching.funFact: GCC and Clang can auto-vectorize range-based for loops — converting your loop into SIMD instructions that process 4-8 elements simultaneously. Your clean range-based for might compile to faster code than a manual index loop! ⚡

mcq.q: for(const auto& x : v) vs for(auto x : v) — key difference?
options: const auto& reads by reference (no copy, can't modify); auto makes a copy
auto& modifies; const auto& doesn't compile
mcq.explanation: Performance: auto x copies each element — O(element size) per iteration. const auto& x binds a reference — O(1), no copy. For strings and vectors, const auto& is significantly faster.

mcq.q: for(auto& n:{1,2,3}) n*=2; — does this modify the initializer list?
options: No — initializer lists are const, compile error with auto&
mcq.explanation: Initializer List: {1,2,3} creates a const initializer_list. You cannot bind non-const auto& to const elements — compile error. Use a vector if you need to modify.

mcq.q: Nested: outer 4 iters × inner 3 iters. Total body runs?
while(true) in C++ compared to while(1):
options: They're functionally identical but while(true) is preferred in C++ for clarity
while(1) is C++, while(true) is C
mcq.explanation: Equivalent: Both are infinite loops. while(true) is preferred in C++ because true is the bool literal — clearer intent than the integer 1.

coding.title: Boss: Efficient read loop
coding.objective: for(___ s : strings) — what prefix reads by reference without copy?
coding.hint: const = can't modify, auto& = reference (no copy). Together: const auto&.

coding.title: Boss: Range sum

options: auto s copies each string — use: for(const auto& s : words) to avoid copying
debugging.answer: auto s copies each string — use: for(const auto& s : words) to avoid copying
debugging.hint: String copy is expensive. const auto& binds a reference — no copy, same result, much faster for large strings.

prediction.explanation: Find max: starts at 5, 8>5→8, 9>8→9. Final hi=9.
